# UC-19.1.37 · Proxmox vzdump Scheduled Backup Task Failure Detection

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Availability, Reliability &middot; **Wave:** Walk &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We check whether the overnight copies of your virtual machines actually finished, or stopped halfway without anyone noticing. If those copies fail quietly, a bad day on the servers can also become a bad day for getting your systems back, so we raise it while the window to fix the backup is still open.*

---

## Description

Consumes PVE `cluster/tasks` history rows in `sourcetype=proxmox:task:vzdump` (scripted API poll) where `type` is vzdump, normalises host and id fields with `coalesce(node,host)` and `coalesce(id,vmids,vmid)` because the API stores guest lists in `id`, maps the lowercased PVE `status` string to `reason_norm` buckets (`storage_full`, `timeout`, `auth_failed`, `qmp_quiesce_failed`, `target_unreachable`, `lock_contention`, `snapshot_collision`, else `other_error`), drops healthy `ok` and in-flight `running` rows, estimates duration from `endtime`−`starttime` when both exist, and rolls up the last 24 h per `node`+`vmids` with `stats` to emit `last_failure`, `failures_24h`, `reasons`, `raw_status_msgs`, and `last_upid`, then classifies a `severity_bucket` where repeated counts elevate priority and certain buckets force `high` even on first failure. Complements, but does not require, the multiline per-job `proxmox:vzdump:job` stream for the same window.

## Value

The organisation’s recoverability story is built on a schedule people rarely watch: nightly or weekly `vzdump` runs to Proxmox Backup Server, ZFS, NFS, or CIFS. A silent failure there leaves finance, legal, and product leadership assuming a ransomware restore or corruption event can be bound by an agreed recovery point, when in fact the last good image is older than policy or missing for a tier-one guest. Auditors and cyber insurers increasingly ask for evidence that backups completed, not only that a policy document exists. Surfacing non-OK task rows, correlated guest ids, and a stable `upid` for triage gives the platform and storage teams a time-bounded way to open the right runbook, expand the right datastore, fix PBS authentication, or reschedule contention-heavy guests before a real outage turns a bad weekend into a bad quarter. The business outcome is fewer undetected RPO breaches, fewer “we thought it was backed up” surprises during table-top exercises, and a defensible log trail for boards that ask when backup last succeeded.

## Implementation

Deploy `proxmox-monitoring` on a forwarder that can call the PVE API with a token, enable the scripted input to `sourcetype=proxmox:task:vzdump`, add optional `monitor` for `/var/log/vzdump/*.log`, load `proxmox_backup_jobs.csv` as a lookup, save the `spl` as a scheduled search with a 5–15 minute cadence, throttle on `node`+`vmids`, and wire drilldowns to the PVE task and PBS UIs; extend later with a second search over `proxmox:vzdump:job` for `ERROR:` text.

## Evidence

Saved search `proxmox_vzdump_task_failures_24h` (or your name) in `proxmox-monitoring`, scheduled every 5–15 minutes, plus a dashboard that lists `node`, `vmids`, `severity_bucket`, and `last_upid`, retained per your internal evidence policy.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites: Proxmox VE 7.x or 8.x with scheduled vzdump jobs (Datacenter UI or `jobs.cfg`), a Splunk Universal or Heavy Forwarder that can open HTTPS to the PVE API on 8006 (or your site proxy), Python 3 for a small poller, and a dedicated `virtualization` index. No vendor Splunkbase add-on fully covers Proxmox; you own an app such as `proxmox-monitoring` that contains scripted input, `props.conf`, and lookup tables. Polling the cluster task API returns structured `status` and time fields; tailing per-job `vzdump` log files under `/var/log/vzdump` returns line-level `ERROR:` text. The saved search in this use case is written against the API-shaped sourcetype; add a second detection later that parses `proxmox:vzdump:job` if you need reason strings not present in the short task row.

## Step 1 Onboard Proxmox vzdump Telemetry

You need at least one primary path, and a second is strongly recommended. Path A: scripted input (recommended) on a host that can reach the API, which calls `GET /api2/json/cluster/tasks` with a `typefilter` that includes `vzdump` and, depending on PVE build, a `source` that covers active plus archived task rows (examples include `user` and `archive`—read `pveproxy` and `pvedaemon` help on your version before you hard-code query parameters). Store a per-forwarder `since` watermark in a small file under `var` so each poll only requests new history instead of the full list. Create a `splunk@pve` (or security-approved) user, grant token auth, and give it rights that can read tasks and storage metadata without being `root@pam` interactive: typical combinations include `Datastore.Audit` for visibility into backup targets, `VM.Backup` for job-level contract, and `Sys.Audit` for cluster context. The HTTP header is `Authorization: PVEAPIToken user@pam!tokenid=secret`. Path B: `monitor` stanza for `/var/log/vzdump/*.log` on each PVE node where guests run, because the job file records `INFO: starting backup`, per-guest `ERROR:` with message text, and `INFO: backup job finished with errors` lines the API can collapse into a shorter `status` string. Path C (optional) is a narrow `imfile` or UF tail on journald or `/var/log/syslog` for `tag=vzdump`; use it only for coarse "daemon started" context because per-VM detail still lives in Path B for failures.

`inputs.conf` (adjust paths to `$SPLUNK_HOME` for forwarders):

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/local/inputs.conf
# Universal Forwarder on PVE node OR Heavy Forwarder polling cluster API

[script:///opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/bin/poll_vzdump_tasks.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 300
sourcetype = proxmox:task:vzdump
index = virtualization
source = proxmox:rest_api:tasks

[monitor:///var/log/vzdump/*.log]
disabled = 0
sourcetype = proxmox:vzdump:job
index = virtualization
host_segment = 3
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

Wrapper `poll_vzdump_tasks.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Wrapper exports API credentials from Splunk credential store, then invokes Python
PVE_API_TOKEN=$(${SPLUNK_HOME}/bin/splunk show passwd -auth admin:changeme 2>/dev/null | grep proxmox_api_token | awk '{print $NF}')
PVE_API_HOST="pve-cluster.internal:8006"
export PVE_API_TOKEN PVE_API_HOST
exec /usr/bin/python3 /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/bin/poll_vzdump_tasks.py
```

The Python poller should request tasks, map each `data[]` object to a single minified JSON line, preserve `id` exactly as the API returns (often a comma-list of guest ids for `type=vzdump`), and not invent a `task_type` field—PVE already sets `type`. Point `_time` to `starttime` when the field is seconds since epoch; if the forwarder timezones drift, also index `_poll_time` for forensics. Document TLS verification (many labs use a private CA; the forwarder must trust the chain).

## Step 2 Parse and Normalize

`props.conf` should treat API lines as single-line JSON with time bound to `starttime` seconds, and treat job logs as multiline job sessions so operators can search `ERROR:` without drowning in one-line-per-byte noise.

```ini
[proxmox:task:vzdump]
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
KV_MODE = none
TIME_PREFIX = "starttime"\s*:\s*
TIME_FORMAT = %s
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
category = Structured
disabled = false
description = Proxmox VE vzdump backup task records polled from /api2/json/cluster/tasks

[proxmox:vzdump:job]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = true
BREAK_ONLY_BEFORE = ^INFO:\s+starting\s+new\s+backup\s+job
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)(?=INFO:\s+starting\s+new\s+backup\s+job)
TIME_PREFIX = ^INFO:\s+
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 64
TRUNCATE = 0
EXTRACT-vmid = (?:VM|CT)\s+(?<vmid>\d+)
EXTRACT-status_line = ERROR:\s+Backup\s+of\s+(?:VM|CT)\s+(?<failed_vmid>\d+)\s+failed\s+-\s+(?<error_reason>.+)$
description = Per-job vzdump output log written to /var/log/vzdump/{qemu,lxc}-VMID.log
```

SPL at search time coalesces `vmids = coalesce(id,vmids,vmid)` because API rows name the guest list as `id` for vzdump tasks, not `vmid` alone. The `reason_norm` `case()` maps the lowercased PVE `status` string to a short machine bucket so dashboards stay stable when Proxmox slightly rephrases the same issue between releases. The lookup ties human job labels to RPO: keep it current when `/etc/pve/jobs.cfg` or the UI changes storage targets.

```ini
# /opt/splunk/etc/apps/proxmox-monitoring/lookups/proxmox_backup_jobs.csv
job_id,schedule,target_storage,retention,owner_team,sla_rpo_hours
backup-prod-daily,daily 02:00,pbs-prod,keep-daily=7;keep-weekly=4,platform-eng,24
backup-dr-weekly,sat 04:00,pbs-dr,keep-weekly=8;keep-monthly=6,dr-team,168
backup-dev-weekly,sun 06:00,nfs-dev,keep-weekly=4,dev-platform,168
```

Register the CSV in `transforms.conf` and expose it in Settings → Lookups, then you can `lookup proxmox_backup_jobs vmids OUTPUT` after you `makemv` the comma list—many sites prefer to join in a second `rex` to split the string.

## Step 3 Validate

These checks are specific to backup tasks, not generic indexer health. (a) On a PVE node: `pvesh get /cluster/tasks --typefilter vzdump --output-format json | jq` (flags vary—use `pvesh` help) and confirm you see a non-empty `data` array with the fields this UC expects, including a recent `UPID:...:vzdump:...` row when a window ran. (b) On the same node: `tail -f /var/log/vzdump/qemu-100.log` (replace 100) while a job runs, and watch for `INFO: starting` and a terminal success or `ERROR:` line. (c) In Splunk: `index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:vzdump" earliest=-1h | head 5` and expand fields—`node`, `id`, `status`, `upid` must be present. (d) Negative test in a non-production job: point a one-guest test backup at a deliberately wrong PBS data store name or an unreachable mount; expect a non-OK `status` in the next poll. Roll the bad setting back and confirm the row recovers. (e) If you also ingest per-job files, `index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:vzdump:job" "ERROR:" earliest=-7d` should return human-readable text that matches the API failure string for the same time window. (f) Clock skew: compare `_time` on an event to the Proxmox task UI; if the gap exceeds your tolerance, move `MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD` or the forwarder NTP config before tuning alert thresholds. (g) Permissions: a token missing `VM.Backup` can yield empty or 403; fix RBAC, not the SPL.

## Step 4 Operationalize

**Operator workflow:** When the alert pages, the on-call platform engineer opens the Proxmox UI to Datacenter → Tasks, filters to failed `vzdump` rows, and copies the `UPID` to compare with the `last_upid` field. Cross-check the guest id list against inventory, then read the per-job `vzdump` log for the `ERROR:` line. If the target is Proxmox Backup Server, open the PBS web UI to the named datastore; confirm whether free space, chunk verify, or token expiry explains the string you saw, without confusing backup failure with the separate GC job. For quick retries, a documented command path is to run a manual `vzdump` for a single `vmid` to the same storage to prove whether the failure is reproducible, after confirming the guest is not under live migration. Use drilldown search tokens from the alert to a dashboard that pre-fills `index=virtualization` on both `proxmox:task:vzdump` and `proxmox:vzdump:job`, with `earliest` bound to the failure window. Deep links: PVE task history `https://<pve-host>:8006/?#v1:0:tasks` and PBS `https://<pbs-host>:8007/#DataStore-pbs-prod` (change names to your site).

**Escalation policy:** A single `medium` from one missed window in a test cluster may go to a ticket; three or more `failures_24h` for the same `node`+`vmids` pair is treated as a chronic regression and should page the platform-eng rota. Any row where `reason_norm` maps to `storage_full` or `auth_failed` should also page storage on-call, because the fix may be a datastore expansion or a token rotation, not a guest reboot. Throttle the alert: one open incident per `node`+`vmids` per hour, with optional suppression if your change calendar shows a planned PBS upgrade that temporarily rejects writes. Do not silence the entire class because one lab guest was intentionally left broken for training.

**Silencing and drilldowns:** If your backup window deliberately overlaps a storage firmware update, load a `maintenance_windows` lookup (node FQDN + time range) and route events to a lower priority queue instead of dropping them. The drilldown to Datacenter task history and PBS datastore pages should be embedded in the alert body so a bridge does not start from a blank search. Optional: a secondary panel that `append`s a search over `proxmox:vzdump:job` for the same `vmid` to show the `ERROR:` text when API rows are short.

## Step 5 Continuous Improvement

**Threshold tuning (first 30 days):** Watch how often `lock_contention` and `qmp_quiesce_failed` appear without a second-day repeat; many of those clear when the next backup runs outside the heavy batch hour. If your organisation accepts one retry, demote a single-event `lock_contention` from a page to a ticket after you have thirty days of evidence. Conversely, if storage teams report that `target_unreachable` always precedes a router misconfiguration, raise that bucket’s routing rule to a faster queue. Revisit the `case()` mapping whenever Proxmox changes error strings in release notes, and keep a short appendix of raw `status` samples per major version.

**Extensions:** Add a parallel scheduled search against PBS if you have JSON poll rights to `/api2/json/admin/datastore` or similar usage endpoints, and plot free-space trend lines next to this UC so late-night `storage_full` does not surprise capacity planning. If you run Splunk ITSI, you can key service entities on `node`+`vmids` and attach this alert as a dependent KPI, but keep the source-of-truth fields aligned with the `lookup` row for RPO. For automation, a SOAR play can open a change pre-filled with the `upid`, guest list, and last error, but the human should still read the per-job `vzdump` file before a destructive retry on production guests.

**Primary saved search (paste into Saved Searches, keep identical to the `spl` field in this use case file):**

```spl
index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:vzdump" earliest=-24h@h
| eval node = coalesce(node, host)
| eval vmids = coalesce(id, vmids, vmid)
| eval status_norm = lower(coalesce(status, ""))
| where status_norm != "ok" AND status_norm != "running" AND isnotnull(status)
| eval reason_norm = case(
    match(status_norm, "(no space left|enospc|out of space)"), "storage_full",
    match(status_norm, "(got timeout|operation timed out|deadline exceeded)"), "timeout",
    match(status_norm, "(authentication failed|permission denied|forbidden)"), "auth_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(qmp command|guest-fsfreeze|qga not running)"), "qmp_quiesce_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(connection failed|no route to host|cifs mount|nfs mount)"), "target_unreachable",
    match(status_norm, "(vm is locked|got lock timeout)"), "lock_contention",
    match(status_norm, "(duplicate snapshot|snapshot name)"), "snapshot_collision",
    1==1, "other_error")
| eval duration_sec = if(isnotnull(endtime) AND isnotnull(starttime), endtime - starttime, null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_failure,
        count as failures_24h,
        values(reason_norm) as reasons,
        values(status) as raw_status_msgs,
        latest(duration_sec) as last_duration_sec,
        latest(upid) as last_upid
        by node, vmids
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    failures_24h >= 3, "critical",
    mvfind(reasons, "storage_full") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "auth_failed") >= 0, "high",
    failures_24h >= 1, "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| sort -failures_24h, +severity_bucket
| table last_failure, node, vmids, severity_bucket, failures_24h, reasons, raw_status_msgs, last_duration_sec, last_upid
```

Narrative for reviewers: the `where` block removes healthy `ok` and in-flight `running` tasks so the table lists only completed anomalies. The `stats` step aggregates by `node` and the coalesced guest list key so a repeated failure across retries in the lookback window raises `failures_24h` above 1, which the `severity_bucket` logic treats as more serious than a single blip, except that storage- or auth-class reasons already warrant `high` even once. The `table` is suitable for a dashboard and for alert mail bodies; for streaming analytics on consecutive nights, clone the search and add a `streamstats` pass upstream of `stats` in a second draft once you have enough volume to validate streak detection without double-counting maintenance windows.


## SPL

```spl
index=virtualization sourcetype="proxmox:task:vzdump" earliest=-24h@h
| eval node = coalesce(node, host)
| eval vmids = coalesce(id, vmids, vmid)
| eval status_norm = lower(coalesce(status, ""))
| where status_norm != "ok" AND status_norm != "running" AND isnotnull(status)
| eval reason_norm = case(
    match(status_norm, "(no space left|enospc|out of space)"), "storage_full",
    match(status_norm, "(got timeout|operation timed out|deadline exceeded)"), "timeout",
    match(status_norm, "(authentication failed|permission denied|forbidden)"), "auth_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(qmp command|guest-fsfreeze|qga not running)"), "qmp_quiesce_failed",
    match(status_norm, "(connection failed|no route to host|cifs mount|nfs mount)"), "target_unreachable",
    match(status_norm, "(vm is locked|got lock timeout)"), "lock_contention",
    match(status_norm, "(duplicate snapshot|snapshot name)"), "snapshot_collision",
    1==1, "other_error")
| eval duration_sec = if(isnotnull(endtime) AND isnotnull(starttime), endtime - starttime, null())
| stats latest(_time) as last_failure,
        count as failures_24h,
        values(reason_norm) as reasons,
        values(status) as raw_status_msgs,
        latest(duration_sec) as last_duration_sec,
        latest(upid) as last_upid
        by node, vmids
| eval severity_bucket = case(
    failures_24h >= 3, "critical",
    mvfind(reasons, "storage_full") >= 0 OR mvfind(reasons, "auth_failed") >= 0, "high",
    failures_24h >= 1, "medium",
    1==1, "low")
| sort -failures_24h, +severity_bucket
| table last_failure, node, vmids, severity_bucket, failures_24h, reasons, raw_status_msgs, last_duration_sec, last_upid
```

## Visualization

(1) Single value: count of `severity_bucket` in {critical, high} for the time picker. (2) Table: `last_failure`, `node`, `vmids`, `severity_bucket`, `failures_24h`, `reasons`, `last_upid`, linked drilldowns to a raw search. (3) Timechart: stacked count of `failures_24h` by `node`. (4) Optional panel: side-by-side `proxmox:vzdump:job` search filtered by `vmid` for `ERROR:` lines. (5) Map panel if you have geo: optional; not required. (6) **Drill** token passes `node`+`vmids` into a child search with `earliest` matched to the alert.

## Known False Positives

•

- A planned PVE or PBS upgrade with documented backup pause: the task list may show `ERROR` for one window; suppress when the change record names both endpoints.
•
- A short `WARNINGS` with a completed exit where the business still has a restorable image—treat WARNINGS as real but not always P1; read the per-job `vzdump` file before paging storage.
•
- A guest you intentionally powered off for maintenance: the job may mark `vm is locked` or a snapshot pause string until it returns; that is an inventory signal, not a storage outage.
•
- **pvesr** or storage migration jobs that temporarily lock a disk can make the next `vzdump` look like `lock_contention` every night until migration completes—triage with migration status, not a blind ticket storm.
•
- A lab `vzdump` to a full scratch datastore used to test alerting: the `storage_full` event is a validation pass, not production capacity work.
•
- A duplicate `upid` re-indexed after you rotated Splunk cert or reinstalled a forwarder—dedupe in alert post-processing on `node`+`vmids`+`last_upid`+`_cd` to avoid a duplicate page.
•
- A narrow NFS blip on a 100 Mbit management path that recovers before your retry: one `timeout` may be acceptable; look for 3+ `failures_24h` or extend lookback in the chronic clone search.
•
- A PBS token rotation on schedule where the PVE `Datastore` owner forgot to update the PVE side first—first failure is an auth class page; after rotation succeeds, the next window should be clean or you miswired two systems.
